title: Roman carved letter proportions
subtitle: How the geometric construction of Trajan column capitals defines calligraphy letterform widths
excerpt: The inscribed capitals on the Trajan column in Rome are the oldest surviving fully realised system of Roman capital proportions. Every modern broad-edge and brush-lettered Roman capital traces its width relationships back to this inscription. Understanding the geometric system behind the letters explains why an N looks wrong when it is the same width as an O.
sourceNotes: Edward Johnston, Writing and Illuminating and Lettering (1906), chapter on Roman capitals and the proportional system. The Trajan column itself is ancient; the geometric analysis of its letterforms is documented extensively in Johnston and in Edward Catich, The Origin of the Serif (1968, copyright). The proportional system described here follows Johnston's public-domain analysis.

glossaryTerms.definition: The inscribed Roman capital letterforms on the Trajan column (AD 113), used as the canonical reference for Roman capital proportions in calligraphy.
The relationship between a letter's width and its height, which varies systematically across the alphabet: O is nearly square; I is narrow; M and W are wider than square.
The small terminal stroke at the end of a main stroke in a Roman letter. In carved inscriptions, the serif is formed by the final chisel pass broadening the stroke end.

content.text: The proportions of the Roman capital alphabet are not arbitrary. They come from a geometric system based on the circle and the square. Letters are grouped by width into roughly four categories. Understanding the system matters because it explains why letters in the same inscription look consistent even though each one has a different width.
The four width groups
The
Trajan capitals
sort into four width groups based on the
letter width ratio
Full-width (nearly square): O, Q, C, G, D. These letters derive from a circle inscribed in a square; their width is very close to their height.
Three-quarter width: A, H, K, N, T, U, V, X, Y, Z. Width roughly three-quarters of height.
Half-width (narrow): B, E, F, J, L, P, R, S. Width roughly half of height, with internal counters arranged in stacked or projecting bowls.
Over-width (wider than square): M, W. These letters exceed the square width because of their multiple diagonals.
Thin: I, J. These stand alone as near-hairline stems with serifs.
The serif and its function
In the Trajan inscription, the
serif

is a product of the incising process: the broad flat chisel that cut the main strokes was worked back at the ends to produce a small triangular bracket that stops the eye at the letter's terminal. In calligraphy this is approximated by a serif stroke added at the start and end of each stem. The serif is not merely decorative; it creates the visual stop that makes a row of Roman capitals read as a line.
Why this matters in calligraphy practice

If the width groups are unknown, all capitals get written the same width. H may look fine but O will look too narrow. Knowing the four groups before starting means each letter gets the right width. The groups apply to Foundational caps, carved Roman letters, and large display work.
Worked example: setting out MAN

Suppose you are writing the word MAN at a cap-height of 50 mm. M is over-width: allow 60 mm. A is three-quarter-width: allow 37 mm. N is three-quarter-width: allow 37 mm. Total baseline span: 134 mm plus inter-letter spacing. If all three letters were set at 37 mm, M would look crushed; at 37 mm each, the word would look like three narrow letters rather than a naturally proportioned Roman inscription.
Cross-references
The Roman capitals tutorial covers stroke order and nib work for these letters. The Foundational capitals tutorial uses the same width groups at a 30-degree nib angle.
